corpus.title: Hungarian PM’s Presidential Gambit Fails as Polgar Declines Nomination
corpus.signal_text:
Hungarian PM’s Presidential Gambit Fails as Polgar Declines Nomination
“The responsibility is entirely mine,” Hungarian Prime Minister Peter Magyar admitted on Monday night, after he had failed to convince the world’s greatest female chess player of all time, Judit Polgar, to accept the nomination to be the country’s next president.
“I don’t feel the strength to take on the historic responsibility, therefore I am unable to accept the invitation,” Polgar, 49, posted last night, ending a day of intense speculation in Hungary and abroad.
Nominating a grandmaster as Hungary’s next president was just the latest in a series of unconventional moves by the government headed by Magyar’s Tisza party. It was a hasty decision by a government that seems to be under constant time pressure to deliver on the numerous promises it made during the campaign for the April 12 election, which it won by a landslide.
“The selection of Hungary’s next president should not be rushed, Peter Magyar is acting as if there were no time for a meaningful public debate about the country’s next head of state,” the human rights watchdog Amnesty International said in a statement.
Tamas Sulyok was regarded as an ideological presidential appointee of former prime minister Viktor Orban. According to Sulyok’s critics, he stood by as the previous government weakened democratic institutions and undermined the rule of law. As a result, voters had lost confidence in him, Magyar argued during the campaign, and his removal would be an essential step in rebuilding democracy and the system of checks of balances in Hungary.
Sulyok refused to budge for weeks until a constitutional amendment was passed on July 13 by the Tisza-dominated parliament which, among other measures, term-limited MPs to 12 years, pensioned off constitutional court judges at 70 and, in a single sentence, terminated the president’s mandate with immediate effect. Sulyok announced on Saturday he would sign the constitutional amendment, “fulfilling my obligation under the Fundamental Law [Hungary’s constitution] after carefully weighing my legal options and my conscience”.
In a lengthy video posted on Facebook, the outgoing president lamented the “end of constitutionalism” and warned that the Tisza government posed a threat to Hungarian democracy. The president has a largely ceremonial role but can block some legislation and delay appointments.

corpus.title: “NO!” PM Magyar Left Red-faced after Rejection from His Presidential Choice
corpus.signal_text:
Thirty-six years after gaining freedom from under the Soviet yoke, Hungary slides back into dictatorship.Continue reading
Hungary’s autocratic leader faces public humiliation as his choice as an interim president replacing the undemocratically removed Tamás Sulyok rejects his offer. Chess grandmaster Judit Polgár has made it clear that she does not want to become the president of a deeply divided nation.
Only weeks after the cringe-worthy scenes at the NATO Summit in Ankara on July 7, when he unsuccessfully tried but failed to shake hands with the U.S. President, Péter Magyar has run into another humiliation of his own device. On Monday, a day after the controversial forced removal of President Tamás Sulyok that commenters dubbed “political violence”, he approached famous Hungarian chess grand master Judit Polgár to take his place as interim president until the new amendment of the constitution takes place. Hours passed by and the nation was holding its breath, as it slowly became clear that Judit Polgár may not want to fill the role so comprehensively discredited by the politically motivated campaign against the previous president. Finally, late in the afternoon, the news broke that she will not accept Magyar’s offer.
In her explanation on social media she wrote that “I consider it the greatest honor of my life to have been nominated by the initiators for the office of President of the Republic, and I am deeply grateful for this. I greatly appreciate the Prime Minister’s gesture in nominating an independent candidate who stands above party politics for the office of President of the Republic, despite his party’s significant parliamentary influence.”
At the same time, I do not feel I have the strength to take on the historic responsibility of uniting a divided nation, and so I am unable to accept the nomination.”
“I will do everything in my power to support a new, independent, and respected President of the Republic in achieving these goals”, closed her words Judit Polgár.
A spectacular celebration – and a very special game with @magyarpeterMP!

Chess champion Polgar turns down offer to be Hungary’s president

Chess legend Judit Polgar on Monday turned down Hungarian Prime Minister Peter Magyar’s offer to join his push to break with the Viktor Orban era as the country’s president. The pro-European conservative had nominated Polgar, widely considered the greatest woman to have taken to the chequered board, as a figure from outside politics to help carry out his promise of “regime change” from Orban’s hardline 16-year tenure. “I do not feel I have the strength to take on the historic responsibility of..
